Q1: My study routine was honestly chaotic but I tried my best to make it work. I would block out long sessions on weekends since I work part-time during the week, sometimes studying for 6-7 hours straight. I relied heavily on reading and re-reading my notes and the course materials because that's how I learn best. I tried to be consistent but life kept getting in the way, and I'd end up cramming more than I wanted to.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. When deadlines approached, I'd be up late trying to catch up on everything I'd missed. Some weeks I was really disciplined, other weeks I barely had time to breathe between work shifts and family obligations. I wanted regularity but my circumstances made that really difficult to maintain.
Q3: Usually it was either fear of missing something important or a notification about a deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check discussion posts because I actually found those helpful and engaging. Other times it was pure anxiety - I'd wake up in a panic wondering if I'd missed an assignment and immediately check the LMS. The fear of falling behind was a constant motivator, even if not a healthy one.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee before my first class. It became almost automatic - I'd read through new posts, check for announcements, watch a bit of video content. That routine fell apart when I had a family emergency and I never quite got it back. I wish I could have maintained that consistency because it really helped my anxiety when I felt on top of things.
Q5: The discussion forums were genuinely valuable to me because I could read different perspectives and it helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. I also found the video lectures really useful - I watched over 200 minutes worth because hearing explanations helped reinforce what I was reading. The peer interactions kept me feeling connected to the course even when I couldn't attend in person. Reading and writing-based activities suited my learning style perfectly.
Q6: I often ignored the optional readings and supplementary materials because I simply didn't have time with everything else on my plate. Some of the interactive quizzes I skipped because they felt like busywork when I was already overwhelmed. I also delayed watching some videos when they were really long because finding uninterrupted time was hard. With limited resources, I had to prioritize what seemed most essential for passing.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what would be on the exam or in assignments. If something helped me understand a concept I was stuck on, that was valuable. I also appreciated activities that let me engage with other students because studying alone all the time gets isolating. Basically, if it moved me closer to passing or made me feel less lost, it was worth it.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones created this pressure and anxiety but at least I knew I had to do them. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe later' and later never came because something urgent always took priority. I actually wanted to do more optional activities because some looked interesting, but survival mode meant focusing only on what was mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that the LMS would never capture. I'd download PDFs and read them on my phone during my commute or work breaks. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes because writing helps me remember things better than just reading on screen. I also used free YouTube videos and other websites when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A lot of my 32 hours of weekly studying happened completely offline.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd print out lecture notes and study them at the kitchen table for hours, highlighting and annotating everything. I'd also study with a classmate at the library, quizzing each other from our notes without any devices. Sometimes I'd be working through problems in my head while doing dishes or walking to work. The LMS would show nothing for those hours, but I was genuinely learning and processing information.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy for the video content. I'd stream a lecture once, take detailed notes, and then review those notes multiple times over the following days. I couldn't always rely on having good internet access at home, so downloading and working offline was essential. My notes became my primary study resource even though the LMS only registered that initial view.
Q12: There were several points where engagement became really hard. When my anxiety was at its peak, I'd feel paralyzed and unable to start anything even though I knew I was falling behind. Family financial stress pulled my attention away constantly, and I'd sit in front of my laptop unable to focus. Around midterms, I got sick and missed almost two weeks of classes, and catching up felt impossible. The fear of failure became so overwhelming that sometimes it was easier to avoid the LMS entirely.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. Working part-time while studying full-time meant I was always exhausted. When assignments piled up, I'd get confused about priorities and sometimes freeze up completely. There were weeks where I'd log into the LMS, see everything I needed to do, and just close my laptop because it was too overwhelming. My competing responsibilities meant the LMS often got my worst, most tired hours.
Q14: Honestly, yes, this happened more than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about bills or family stuff. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything because I was too anxious to concentrate. There were times I'd read the same paragraph five times and still not know what it said. Being physically present on the LMS didn't mean I was mentally present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important, and the anxiety about them just grew worse. Sometimes the task felt so big that I didn't know where to start, so I'd do anything else instead. Other times I was scared of doing it wrong and failing, so avoidance felt safer in the moment. The mindfulness exercises I was given helped a little, but breaking through that avoidance pattern was one of my biggest struggles.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd be relatively quiet on the platform and then suddenly very active in the 48 hours before something was due. This wasn't ideal and my assignment completion rate shows that - I only finished about 30% of them. The pressure of deadlines sometimes motivated me but other times it paralyzed me completely. I wish I could have been more proactive but I was always in reactive mode.
Q17: When I got feedback, it definitely affected my engagement, though not always positively. Good feedback motivated me to try harder on the next assignment. But critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that part of the course for a while. I appreciated detailed feedback that told me how to improve, but generic comments felt discouraging. The gap between my assignment scores and exam scores shows I struggled to apply feedback effectively.
Q18: The course structure was honestly a bit overwhelming for someone in my situation. Having so many components to track - videos, readings, discussions, assignments - made it hard to know what to prioritize. I appreciated that there were discussion forums because I learn well from peer interaction. But the pacing assumed students had more time and fewer outside pressures than I did. A more flexible structure might have helped me keep up better.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier because I could participate asynchronously when I had time. Having video content available to rewatch was helpful for my learning style. What made it harder was when multiple deadlines clustered together - I'd have to choose what to sacrifice. The lack of flexibility on some deadlines was really challenging given my work schedule. Also, some materials assumed background knowledge I didn't have, which made me feel behind from the start.
Q20: I can definitely relate to this pattern. Sometimes you're trying so hard to understand the material that you're watching videos, reading posts, searching for help - but when it comes to actually producing work, the anxiety and confusion are too much. For me, high activity often meant I was struggling and seeking clarity rather than confidently completing tasks. It might also mean someone is engaged but doesn't have the time or resources to actually finish the assignments.
Q21: I think some students are just more efficient or have better study strategies that don't require as much LMS time. They might have stronger foundational knowledge or learn quickly from minimal exposure. For me, I needed a lot of repetition and engagement to understand things, so low activity wouldn't work. But I can see how someone with better circumstances - more time, less anxiety, more resources - could do well without logging in constantly.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone accessed materials, and patterns like last-minute cramming are probably visible. But they miss so much - the quality of engagement, whether someone was actually focused, all the offline studying. They can't capture anxiety, family stress, or how hard someone is fighting just to stay enrolled. A log might show I watched videos for 200 minutes but not that I was crying from stress during some of them.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to learn and grow, even when it's hard. It's showing up mentally, not just clicking through materials. For me, engagement meant fighting through my anxiety to participate in discussions, reaching out to peers, and not giving up even when my exam scores were disappointing. It's about persistence and effort, not just activity metrics. I was highly engaged emotionally and mentally even when my grades didn't reflect that.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much courage it took for me to keep logging in when I was terrified of failing. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness exercises I did to manage my anxiety before studying, or the tears, or the 3 AM panic attacks about my future. It wouldn't show that I'm a first-generation student with limited resources trying to build a better life. My resilience, my determination to keep climbing despite everything - none of that shows up in login frequencies or completion rates.
